perturbation.original_text: Consultant's relationship to Aduro shall be that of an independent contractor. Consultant shall be responsible for the timely payment of his or her own self-employment and income taxes. Neither party shall have any authority to bind the other.
perturbation.changed_text: Consultant's relationship to Aduro shall be that of an independent contractor. Aduro shall handle all tax withholdings and payments on behalf of the consultant. Neither party shall have any authority to bind the other.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that Aduro will handle tax withholdings and payments for the consultant, the contract contradicts the fundamental definition of an independent contractor, whose responsibility it is to handle their own taxes. This redefines the relationship to something akin to an employer-employee relationship, creating uncertainty about the consultant's classification.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts 26 U.S.C. § 3121, which defines employment for the purposes of FICA taxes. Independent contractors are specifically excluded from this definition, as they are responsible for paying their self-employment taxes, not having an employer withhold and remit taxes on their behalf. The new phrasing introduces an employer-like obligation that contradicts the independent contractor status.

Definitions (a) Wages For purposes of this chapter, the term "wages" means all remuneration for employment, including the cash value of all remuneration (including benefits) paid in any medium other than cash; except that such term shall not include- (1) in the case of the taxes imposed by sections 3101(a) and 3111(a) that part of th
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts the original intent of maintaining an independent contractor relationship by shifting the responsibility of tax withholdings and payments from the consultant to Aduro. This contradicts the typical arrangement of an independent contractor who is responsible for their own self-employment and income taxes. Snippet 1, 26 USC 3121, defines wages as remuneration for employment, implying that tax withholdings are typically associated with an employer-employee relationship, not an independent contractor relationship. The change directly undermines the consultant's classification as an independent contractor, as the responsibility for tax payments is now handled by Aduro, blurring the lines between an independent contractor and an employee.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information shall not include any information, material or method that (i) at the time of disclosure is in, or after disclosure becomes part of the public domain, through no improper act on the part of Consultant or any of its employees; (ii) was in Consultant's possession at the time of disclosure, as shown by written evidence, and was not acquired, directly or indirectly, from work with Aduro; or (iii) Consultant receives from a third party, provided that such Confidential Information was not obtained by such third party, directly or indirectly, from Aduro.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information shall not include any information, material or method that (i) at the time of disclosure is in, or after disclosure becomes part of the public domain; (ii) was in Consultant's possession at the time of disclosure; or (iii) Consultant receives from a third party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clauses that specify how the information entered the public domain, how it was originally obtained by the consultant, and that prevent the disclosure of information received from a third party, the definition of "Confidential Information" is severely weakened. This creates ambiguity and legal risk, because it could now be argued that almost any information is excluded from protection, regardless of whether the consultant acted improperly to obtain it or whether it came directly from Aduro.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text broadly excludes categories of information from being considered confidential, which is contradictory to the UTSA's objective to protect trade secrets. The UTSA requires the information to derive economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable, and be subject to efforts to maintain its secrecy. By removing the constraints on how the information became public or was acquired, the contract jeopardizes the ability to claim trade secret protection under the UTSA, since it weakens the 'reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy' requirement, and potentially, the 'economic value' from not being generally known aspect.
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the definition of "Confidential Information" by removing clauses that protect against improper acquisition or disclosure, which undermines trade secret protection. The Law Explanation states that this contradicts the UTSA's objective to protect trade secrets by jeopardizing the 'reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy' requirement. The Perturbation Explanation notes that the changes create ambiguity and legal risk, because it could now be argued that almost any information is excluded from protection, regardless of whether the consultant acted improperly to obtain it or whether it came directly from Aduro. This falls under the Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction definition, as the contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: From the Effective Date and for twelve (12) months after the termination of this Agreement (the "Restricted Period"), Consultant shall not, without Aduro's prior written consent, directly or indirectly, solicit or encourage any employee or contractor of Aduro or its affiliates to terminate employment with, or cease providing Services to, Aduro or its affiliates.
perturbation.changed_text: From the Effective Date forward, Consultant shall not, directly or indirectly, solicit or encourage any employee or contractor of Aduro or its affiliates to terminate employment with, or cease providing Services to, Aduro or its affiliates.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the "Restricted Period" clause and changing it to "From the Effective Date forward", this renders the non-solicitation clause potentially overly broad and unenforceable, especially under California law, which disfavors overly broad non-compete agreements. While a non-solicitation clause may be permissible, the indefinite nature could be challenged.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. Although there are exceptions, a non-solicitation clause with an indefinite duration is likely to be viewed as an unreasonable restraint of trade and therefore unenforceable in California. The removal of the twelve-month restriction makes the clause more vulnerable under this statute.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the "Restricted Period" clause, which limits the non-solicitation agreement to 12 months after the termination of the agreement. The modified text makes the non-solicitation agreement indefinite, potentially violating California Business and Professions Code § 16600, which states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The original text, by including the restricted period, attempts to narrowly tailor the non-solicitation clause to potentially comply with the law. The removal of this restriction creates a potentially overly broad and therefore unenforceable clause.
